lead: A removals quote can look straightforward at first glance, but the figure on the page is the result of several moving parts, and knowing what sits behind it helps you compare fairly and avoid surprises. As a family-run firm in Westerfield near Ipswich, we have written quotes for Suffolk families since 2005, and we believe a good quote should be clear enough to understand at a glance. Here is how a removals quote is put together, what each part means, and the questions worth asking before you book anyone.

sections.h: Why Quotes Differ from One Firm to Another
sections.body:
The first thing to understand is that two quotes for the same move can quite reasonably differ, and a difference does not automatically mean one is wrong. A quote reflects the size of crew a firm plans to send, the vehicle they intend to use, the time they expect the job to take, and the level of care and protection built in. A figure that looks low may assume a smaller team or fewer hours than the move really needs, which can lead to a stretched, rushed day or extra charges later.

We have always taken the view that the cheapest headline number is not the same as the best value. What matters is whether the quote is built on a true picture of your move, which is why we survey before we price. When you compare quotes, look past the bottom line to what each one actually includes. A clear, slightly higher quote that covers everything can work out far better than a vague, lower one that quietly leaves out packing, materials or protection and then grows on moving day.

sections.h: The Core: Volume, Crew and Vehicle
sections.body:
At the heart of every quote is the volume of your belongings, because that drives almost everything else. The amount you are moving determines how big a van or lorry is needed, how many crew should come, and how many hours the job will take. A modest flat might suit a small team and a single load, while a large family home could call for a bigger crew and more than one trip. This is why we survey first; the volume figure underpins the whole quote, and getting it right keeps the rest accurate.

Crew size is worth understanding in its own right. More hands cost more per hour but finish sooner, and the right balance depends on the access and the volume. A move with long carries or awkward stairs may justify an extra person to keep the day moving safely. The vehicle matters too, since the wrong size means either wasted space or an unwelcome second trip. When you read a quote, it is fair to ask how many crew are coming and what size of vehicle is planned, so you can see the reasoning behind the figure.

Time ties it all together, because in practice you are largely paying for the hours the move takes. Volume, crew, vehicle and access all feed into that estimate. A well-judged quote is one where the planned team and van match the real job, so the day runs to time without either dragging on or feeling frantic. If a quote seems surprisingly low, it is reasonable to wonder whether the crew or hours behind it are realistic for the home you are moving.

sections.h: Distance, Access and Time on the Day
sections.body:
Distance shapes the quote, but in Suffolk it is rarely just miles on a map. What you are really paying for is time on the road, and that depends on the route as much as the distance. A short cross-town move can be slow if parking is awkward at both ends, while a longer run out to the coast or across the county adds genuine hours behind the wheel. A good quote factors in realistic travel for your specific route rather than a flat per-mile rate, which is why two moves of equal distance can fairly differ.

Access feeds into the time element heavily, and it is the factor most often underestimated. Stairs, long carries from the lorry, narrow lanes, parking restrictions and tight doorways all add minutes that mount into hours across a whole house. A quote built after a proper survey will have accounted for the access at both ends, planning around the harder of the two. If a quote was given without anyone seeing or asking about access, treat the figure with some caution, because the assumptions underneath it may not survive the day.

sections.h: What's Included and What Costs Extra
sections.body:
The most important habit when reading a quote is to check exactly what is and is not included. Packing is the big variable: does the price cover a full professional pack, just the loading of boxes you have filled yourself, or something in between? Are boxes, paper and protective materials supplied, or are they a separate cost? Clarity here prevents the most common misunderstanding, where a customer assumes packing is included and discovers on the day that it is not.

Other items to look for include dismantling and reassembling furniture such as beds and wardrobes, disconnecting and reconnecting white goods, and the handling of any large or special items like a piano. Each of these may be built into the quote or listed as an extra, and neither is wrong as long as it is spelled out. We always set these out clearly so you can see what you are paying for, and we would encourage you to ask any firm to do the same. A quote that lists its inclusions plainly is one you can trust and compare with confidence.

sections.h: Storage, Protection and the Small Print
sections.body:
If your move might involve a gap between moving out and moving in, check how storage is handled. It is usually charged separately from the move, generally by the space used and the length of time, and it is far cheaper folded into the plan early than arranged in a panic when a completion date slips. Being family-run with our own secure storage here in Westerfield, we can build a few days or weeks into a quote easily, and we would always rather flag the possibility up front than leave you scrambling later.

Protection is the part of the small print you should never skip. Make sure your move is fully covered, and confirm exactly what that protection means before you book anyone, not after. A reputable quote will state this clearly. It is also worth checking the terms around deposits, cancellation and any conditions tied to the date. None of this is about distrust; it is simply about reading the agreement properly so you know where you stand, just as you would with any service you were paying for.

sections.h: Questions to Ask Before You Book
sections.body:
Before you accept any quote, a handful of questions will tell you almost everything you need to know. Ask whether the quote is based on a survey or simply an estimate over the phone. Ask how many crew and what size of vehicle are planned. Ask precisely what is included, especially around packing and materials, and what would be charged as an extra. Ask how access and travel have been accounted for. And ask, plainly, whether your belongings are fully covered while in their care.

Finally, always get the quote in writing and read it carefully before committing. A written quote is your reference point, and a good firm will be glad to answer questions and explain anything that is unclear rather than rushing you. If your plans change, perhaps you declutter further or the date moves, a fair firm will revise the figure rather than leave you guessing. We are always happy to talk a quote through line by line, because a quote you fully understand is the foundation of a move that runs without nasty surprises.

faqs: Why is one removals quote cheaper than another?

Quotes reflect the crew size, vehicle, time and level of protection a firm plans for. A lower figure may assume a smaller team or fewer hours than your move really needs, which can lead to a rushed day or extra charges. Compare what each quote actually includes, not just the bottom line.
What should be included in a removals quote?

A clear quote should set out the crew and vehicle, the time expected, and exactly which services are covered, such as packing, materials, dismantling furniture, disconnecting appliances and handling special items. It should also confirm that your belongings are fully covered. Always ask what counts as an extra.
Should I get my removals quote in writing?

Yes, always. A written quote is your reference point and spells out what is and is not included, so there is no ambiguity later. A reputable firm will happily provide one and explain anything that is unclear, rather than relying on a verbal figure that is easy to dispute.
Is storage included in a removals quote?

Usually no. Storage is normally charged separately, by the space used and the length of time. If there is any chance of a gap between moving out and in, raise it early so it can be folded into the plan, which is far cheaper than arranging it in a panic if a date slips.
